extracts.extract: If your government, region or city has an open data portal, that should have regular updates. If there's an office of national statistics, sign up for updates. FOI requests are often a good source of data. It's a good idea to have data regularly come to you — either by email or RSS.
extracts.chapter: The scouting mission: where's the data?

extracts.extract: If you are reporting on a specific field like crime, health, education, welfare or the environment, try to find bodies (local, national and international) that publish data regularly in that area.
extracts.chapter: The scouting mission: where's the data?

extracts.extract: Given the choice, always avoid PDFs.

What the data contains doesn't really matter here: the point is the exercises you'll be going through in looking at it. You can apply the same process to most regular public datasets, whether it's employment data, environmental information, or weather.
Building contacts is no less important in data journalism than other forms of journalism: these are the people who can alert you to the existence of data, or even leak it to you.

Every dataset will have stories it can tell. Here are just some typical stories often found in data: top of the pops/flops, holding up a mirror, a freak occurrence, a claim debunked or supported, trend, postcode lottery, juking the stats, victims/winners/losers, things happen together, it's not working.

Spend five minutes looking at the fields (column labels) in your dataset and five minutes going through that list and writing down potential data stories. The dataset alone may not answer your questions — but it may set you on the journey to doing so.

You will often find yourself distracted by many different possible stories in the data — but if you're up against a deadline then you'll need to be focused. Data has so many potential stories that it can be easy to get distracted following multiple avenues of enquiry.

The quickest way to crack open our data and get a good overview of it is by using a pivot table. A pivot table can aggregate figures so we can combine all payments by company to see how much they got in total. Pivot tables can also count things.
extracts.chapter: The crowbar of data journalism: pivot tables

extracts.extract: If you have a dataset it's a good idea to look through the fields and think of possible stories to tell about the subjects. We've narrowed our field to two strong candidates for the 'who' part of our story. The 'what' of the story needs to go into the Values field.
extracts.chapter: Who might your story be about?

extracts.extract: In most cases adding columns to a pivot table will complicate the table to the point where any clarity is lost. Instead, it's generally better to create a new pivot table or use the report filter. Keep things simple wherever you can.
You'll notice that many of these stories don't require any further work on the data — so at this point it's time to make a clean exit, and start speaking to people.
extracts.chapter: Bringing the cream to the top

extracts.extract: Advanced filters allow you to use wildcards to find payments that have something in common. Wildcards allow us to be much more open in our criteria. Look for patterns in your data when filtering — for example, adding a space to exclude false matches like CAR matching CARE.

Searching for cost codes using Google search operators like filetype:xls, filetype:pdf, or site:birmingham.gov.uk can help narrow the search. Sometimes you will find a spreadsheet or document which lists all the codes in your data and adds extra context.
extracts.chapter: Finding related data - search techniques of the professionals

extracts.extract: When making an FOI request relating to data: request digital spreadsheet format (CSV or XLS); ask if different parts of codes have particular meanings; try requesting the data dictionary; anticipate any objections that may be made under exemptions; remember you can request documents as well as data.
extracts.chapter: Finding related data - search techniques of the professionals

extracts.extract: As FOI requests often take weeks or even months, it's worth making these requests even before you plan to chase any stories, so you have basic contextual data available when the next datasets come out.
extracts.chapter: Finding related data - search techniques of the professionals

extracts.extract: Remember to treat data as a source like any other. It is there to be questioned, followed-up and cross-referenced. Data is compiled by humans and published by humans. It is humans who decide when to gather it and how to classify it. All of these decisions influence what the final data looks like.

You mustn't take replies at face value: it's just another source. Nor should you report it as 'he said, she said'. Look for evidence, revisit your data, speak to an independent expert, or whatever will help you be confident which party is correct, and no one is getting hurt unjustifiably.

Once you have your story lead, there's no need to waste more time in the data. Turning that lead into a story means adding context, colour, and people. The five Ws and a H are useful starting points for reaction and explanation.

Before you visualise any data, ask yourself whether you are telling a story about comparison, composition, distribution, or relationships. The key to clear data visualisation is to strip the data down to bare essentials — try to avoid having more than four or five data points. Use one colour for your focal point and another for everything else.

Your story lead may have come from data, but the story itself doesn't have to have numbers — and it should certainly have people. What makes us care about it is reading about the woman who has been unemployed for a year or the couple who had to sell their house. Never forget that any data you work with is a means to an end, not the end itself.
extracts.chapter: Why does it matter - why do I care?

extracts.extract: A good story answers two questions in its first few pars: why does this story matter, and why do I care? The impact is the key piece of information here.
extracts.chapter: Why does it matter - why do I care?

extracts.extract: Consider building relationships with FOI officers, data analysts, statisticians, academics and others who work with data directly and can explain the problems with it.
